journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science

title-group.article-title: Characterization of an atypical Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae serovar 2 isolate with a rough-type lipopolysaccharide

contrib-group.aff: 1)Nippon Institute for Biological Science, Tokyo, Japan
3)Faculty of Agriculture and Aquaculture, University of Cuu Long, Vinh Long, VietNam
4)Medical Mycology Research Center, Chiba University, Chiba, Japan

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: To H: toho@nibs.or.jp, Nippon Institute for Biological Science, 9-2221-1 Shinmachi, Ome, Tokyo 198-0024, Japan

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2023 The Japanese Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p:
This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0:
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: We describe phenotypic and genetic characterization of an atypical Japanese Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae isolate OT761. Nucleotide sequence analysis revealed that gene clusters involved in capsular polysaccharide and O-polysaccharide (O-PS) biosynthesis of the isolate were nearly identical to those of serovar 2 reference strain. The main difference found between the O-PS loci is the shortening of 31 amino acids from the C terminus of WcaJ in the atypical isolate due to a 93 bp deletion at the 3' end of wcaJ gene. Immunoblot analysis revealed that this isolate could not produce O-PS. Taken together, our results showed that the C-terminal domain of the A. pleuropneumoniae WcaJ plays a critical role in enzyme function of WcaJ involved in the biosynthesis of O-PS.

body.p: Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae is the causative agent of porcine pleuropneumonia, an economically important bacterial infection of swine [5]. Isolates can be differentiated into two biovars based on the requirement for nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD-dependent biovar 1; NAD-independent biovar 2) [22]; and subsequently into 19 serovars based mainly on the antigenic diversity of the capsular polysaccharides (CPS) and O-polysaccharide (O-PS) [2, 21, 24]. The prevalent serovars differ by country, region and farm. In Japan, serovar 2 is the most predominant, followed by serovars 1, 5 and 15 [9, 17, 25]. There has also been an increase in unserotypable (UT) isolates that failed to react in the agar gel diffusion (AGD) test using autoclaved antigens (121°C, 1 hr) [17].

Cross-reactions between some serovars (1, 9, and 11; 4 and 7; 3, 6, 8, and 15) and variable results between individual batches of test sera are usually observed in the conventional serotyping tests [14,15,16]. To overcome these limitations, molecular diagnostic methods have been developed for serotyping of A. pleuropneumoniae isolates [1, 2, 8, 28]. These methods allow the determination of the respective serovar and warrant the re-evaluation of previously UT isolates, as well as those that showed serological ambiguities.
We report herein phenotypic and genetic characteristics of this atypical isolate of A. pleuropneumoniae serovar 2.

An A. pleuropneumoniae designated as isolate OT761 was isolated from lung lesions of a dead pig approximately 140 days of age in the Kyushu region, Japan, in 2015. By using a small amount of an A. pleuropneumoniae colony from chocolate blood agar plate as antigen in rapid slide agglutination (RSA) test [16], isolate OT761 showed a very weak reaction with antiserum against serovar 2 reference strain S1536 but no reaction with any of antisera against the reference strains of serovars 1, 3–17. Reaction degree was indicated as +++ (strong positive), ++ (positive), + (positive), w+ (weak positive), ± (very weak), − (negative). A very weak reaction was considered as an ambiguous result. A further four other isolates of A. pleuropneumoniae designed as A, B, C and D isolated from pneumonic lesions of four dead or diseased pigs from the Kanto region, Japan in 2022 were included in this study. They were sent to the Nippon Institute for Biological Science, Ome, Tokyo, Japan for serotyping. A serovar 2 reference strain S1536 was used in the further characterization of these isolates as described below. They were grown at 37°C in the presence of 5% CO2 on chocolate agar (Becton, Dickinson Co., Detroit, MI, USA) as previously described [27].

Serotyping of the five field isolates was then carried out with serovar-specific rabbit antisera prepared against serovar reference strains by using an AGD test with autoclaved antigens (121°C, 1 hr) as described previously [17, 25]. Four isolates A–D and the serovar 2 reference strain S1536, but not isolate OT761, were serotyped as serovar 2 by the AGD test, based on the observation of serovar 2–specific precipitation lines (Fig. 1AFig. 1.
Agar gel diffusion test with rabbit antiserum raised against Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae serovar 2 reference strain S1536. The antiserum against reference strain S1536 (labeled 2S), and antigens of reference strains and field isolates were placed in the center and outer wells, respectively. (A) The test with antigens extracted at 121°C for 1 hr (antoclaved antigens). Serovar 2-specific precipitation lines were seen with serovar 2 reference strain S1536 (labeled 2) and the four isolates (labeled A, B, C, D), but not with isolate OT761 (labeled OT761). (B) The test with antigens extracted at 100°C (heat-extracted antigens). Serovar 2-specific precipitation lines were seen with strain S1536 and isolate OT761, but not with serovar 7 reference strain WF83 (labeled 7).).

Next, we sought to assess whether antigen-extraction temperature could be liable to destroy the capsular antigen. For that purpose, growth on chocolate agar plates (of serovar 2 strains OT761, S1536 or serovar 7 strain WF83) were harvested and divided into 2 portions which were then treated at 100°C [14,15,16] and 121°C [17, 25] for 1 hr each. The serovar 7 reference strain was selected randomly as a representative of core type II strains of A. pleuropneumoniae (serovars 2–5, 7, 8, 10, and 12) [11]. The suspensions were centrifuged at 15,000 × g for 10 min, and the resulting clear supernatants were used as antigens for the AGD test. Precipitation lines were observed for autoclaved (121°C, 1 hr) and heat (100°C, 1 hr)-extracted antigens of serovar 2 reference strain S1536, and for the heat-extracted antigen of isolate OT761 but not for the autoclaved antigen of isolate OT761, and for the autoclaved and heat-extracted antigens of serovar 7 reference strain WF83 (Fig. 1A and 1B). Additionally, we asked whether the higher heat treatment could affect the antigens used for serotyping in the RSA test. The RSA test was carried out using the whole-cell, heat-treated and autoclaved antigens as described previously [16, 17]. The antigens of the serovar 2 reference strain S1536 showed strong positive reactions, whereas the whole-cell and heat-treated (60°C, 2 hr; and 100°C, 1 hr) antigens of isolate OT761 showed only the very weak reactions, and its autoclaved antigen showed a negative reaction with antiserum against S1536 (Fig. 2Fig. 2.
Rapid slide agglutination test with the heat treatment antigens. The antigens of the serovar 2 reference strain S1536S showed strong positive reactions, whereas the whole-cell and heat-treated (60°C, 2 hr; and 100°C, 1 hr) antigens of isolate OT761 showed the very weak reactions, and the autoclaved (121°C, 1hr) antigen of isolate OT761 showed a negative reaction with antiserum against S1536. Reaction degree was indicated as +++ (strong positive), ++ (positive), + (positive), w+ (weak positive), ± (very weak: ambiguous result), − (negative).). Additionally, the antigens of isolate OT761 did not react with any of the serovar-specific rabbit antisera. There was no apparent difference in the reaction between the fresh whole-cell antigens and heat-treated formalized antigens by the RSA test.

Results of the PCR-based apx gene typing [12, 23] and biovar (nadV)-specific PCR tests [2] revealed that five isolates A, B, C, D, and OT761 contained apxIIA, apxIIIA, and apxIVA genes; and a truncated nadV gene specific for biovar 1, as indicated by the lack of a nadV amplicon in PCR and the requirement for NAD for growth. The molecular work and the fact that these field isolates were found to be dependent on NAD in vitro, indicated that the five field isolates were A. pleuropneumoniae biovar 1. Further examination of the genes for the expression, activation, and secretion of Apx toxins by PCR [3] revealed that the isolates of A. pleuropneumoniae contained the same apx gene combination as the serovar 2, 4, 6, 8, and 15 reference strains (an apxIICA, apxIIICA, apxIBD, apxIIIBD, and apxIVA gene pattern). The molecular detection based on the cps gene [1, 2, 8, 24] showed that the five isolates were positive by two cps2-specific PCR tests, having different target genes (cps2E [1], cps2BCD [8], Fig. 3AFig. 3.
Schematic comparison of the capsular polysaccharide (CPS) and O-polysacharide (O-PS) biosynthesis loci of Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae serovar 2 reference strain S1536 and atypical isolate OT761. (A) The CPS locus (17,051 bp) of A. pleuropneumoniae OT761 and comparison to that of strain S1536. The similarity of the CPS loci is 99.6%. The locations of the cps2-specific primers for amplification of cps2E and cps2BCD genes [1, 8] are indicated as small curved black arrows. (B) The O-PS locus (13,372 bp) of A. pleuropneumoniae OT761 and comparison to that (13,465 bp) of A. pleuropneumoniae serovar 2 strain S1536. The similarity of the O-PS loci is 99.9%. The wcaJ gene (1,323 bp) of strain S1536 is shown as a gray arrow. The wcaJ gene of isolate OT761, which is truncated by a 93 bp deletion at nucleotide (nt) positions 1115–1207 of the 3′ end of wcaJ gene (ΔwcaJ, 1,230 bp), is shown as a black arrow. The name of gene is indicated. The numbers above and below the intergenic regions and genes indicate the length in base pairs (bp) of the corresponding regions and genes. The grey blocks between the two sequences each indicate levels of nucleotide identity (light grey=99 to 99.5%, dark grey=99.8 to 100%). ntID and aaID, abbreviation of identities of nucleotide and deduced amino acid sequences, respectively.).

In order to amplify a 1,545 bp amplicon of 16S rDNA, PCR was performed using primer pairs designed from previously published sequences [29] (Table 1Table 1.Primers used in study of Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae isolates
Primer nameSequence (5′ to 3′)Target geneAmplicon size (bp)Reference
AP16SF1ACTTAGCTAAGCAGAATATTGAGCG16S rDNA1,545This study
AP16SR1TGTCGCTCAATTTCTGGTAAGGAG
nadVFCTCACTAAACAAAACTCTGCGTTCnadV1,339[2]
nadRTTCGGATGACAGAACTTTTACCCG
AP2FGAGTGTGATGATGATGCTCTGGTTCcps2E247[1]
AP2RTACCAATAACTGTTGCAACTAACGC
cps2F1-27a)ATGTCAAACATCAACATCCAGAATGCCThe first half of CPS locus8,961This study
APPcps2Rb)CACCCCATGAATCGACTGATTGCCAT[8]
APPcps2Fb)CGCAGCCGGACAAAAACAAATACACGThe second half of CPS locus9,814[8]
cps2R1-17051a)ATGAAAAAAGTATATGTAACGCACGGThis study
lps15F1 (1484)c)ACTCGTAAACGAAGATTCGGGTTATCTTCGCComplete O-PS locus13,371[27]
lps15R2 (1498)CGTGAAAAAGCATCTTTAGCTAAACGTCACGC
CPS=capsular polysaccharide; O-PS=O-polysacchride. a) Primers for amplification of the DNA fragments of the CPS locus were designed on the basis of the sequences of CPS locus of reference strain S1536 (serovar 2, accession ADOE00000000) [29]. b) Primers for amplification of the DNA fragments of the cps2BCD (1,715 bp) genes and the CPS locus [8]. c) Primers for amplification of the O-PS locus [27].). The nucleotide sequence of the 16S rDNA of isolate OT761 (1,515 bp) shared 99.9% identity with those of A. pleuropneumoniae serovars 14, 8, 7, 5, 4, and 3 (accession no. CP031860, CP031866, CP031869, CP063424, CP031873, and CP000687, respectively), whereas it exhibits 100% identity to that of serovar 2 (accession CP031875), revealing that the atypical isolate was more phylogenetically related to serovar 2 than to the other serovars.

In order to elucidate the reason for the discrepancy between molecular and serological typing for the isolate OT761, the gene clusters involved in the biosynthesis of CPS and/or O-PS were PCR-amplified from the isolate OT761, and the four isolates of serovar 2 (A-D). The primer pairs used for amplification of the cps [1, 8, 28] and o-ps [26] genes are listed in Table 1. The PCR was performed as described previously [27]. Purification of PCR products and determination of the nucleotide sequences were performed as described previously [26, 27].

The complete CPS locus (17,051 bp) of isolate OT761 was found between the genes ydeN, encoding a predicted serine hydrolase, and modF, encoding a putative molybdenum transport ATP-binding protein (Fig. 3A). The cps genes of isolate OT761 were found to be 99.6% identical at the nucleotide level with those from serovar 2 reference strain S1536. The complete O-PS locus of isolate OT761 (13,372 bp) identified between the erpA and rpsU genes (Fig. 3B) was found to be 99.9% identical at the nucleotide level with that of serovar 2 strain S1536 (13,465 bp). The sequence analysis of the cps and o-ps genes between serovar 2 strains revealed that there were genetic differences, such as single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs), in the cps2B, cps2C, cps2D, lysA and ydeN genes in the CPS loci, and rmlD gene in the O-PS loci, as well as a 93 bp deletion at the 3′ end of the wcaJ gene in the O-PS locus in isolate OT761, but not in the 1,323 bp wcaJ gene in the reference strain S1536 and four Japanese isolates (A-D). Nucleotide sequences of the 16S rDNA, and cps and o-ps genes of isolate OT761 were deposited in the DDBJ/EMBL/GenBank databases under accessions no. LC708151, LC685098 and LC682597, respectively.

Only missense and silent SNPs were found in the cps and o-ps genes of the atypical isolate. Impact of the SNPs on structure, stability and function of enzymes encoded by the cps2B, cps2C, cps2D, lysA and ydeN genes in CPS biosynthesis was not analyzed in the current study. In the o-ps genes, there was only one missense SNP in the rmlD gene. The nucleotide substitution in turn results in an isoleucine to valine substitution at residue 43 of 291 amino acid RmlD. Data from a previous report suggested that this residue is not involved in the catalytic activity of RmlD [6].

To investigate whether isolate OT761 was defective in O-PS production, the lipopolysaccharide (LPS) of the serovar 2 reference strain (S1536) and five isolates (A, B, C, D and OT761) was extracted and analyzed by sodium dodecyl sulfate–polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) coupled with silver staining or immunoblotting as described previously [7, 27]. The SDS-PAGE profiles of silver-stained LPSs isolated and purified from the serovar 2 reference strain, S1536, and five isolates are shown in Fig. 4.Fig. 4.
Silver-stained sodium dodecyl sulfate–polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis profiles of the purified lipopolysaccharide preparation of Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae strain S1536 and 5 serovar 2 field isolates. Lanes: 1, atypical isolate OT761; 2, S1536 (reference strain); 3–6, field isolates A, B, C and D, respectively. Lane M=molecular mass marker in kilodaltons (kDa). The LPS profiles of strain S1536 and four isolates A, B, C and D showed the presence of a ladder-like banding pattern typical of smooth-type LPS (S-LPS), and a large, deeply staining area representative of the core oligosaccharide and lipid A, whereas that of isolate OT761 lacked the ladder-like SDS-PAGE profile, but a large, deeply staining area representative of the core oligosaccharide and lipid A was detected just trailing the leading edge of the dye front. Isolate OT761 showed only representative LPS profile for rough-type LPS (R-LPS).

The results of immunoblot analysis showed that rabbit antiserum against the serovar 2 reference strain S1536 reacted with long-chain LPS (LC-LPS), giving a ladder-like pattern typical of the S-LPS, with two fast-migrating bands corresponding to the core and lipid A structures in the LPS preparation of the reference serovar 2 strain (Fig. 5Fig. 5.
Immunoblot of the purified lipopolysaccharide preparations of Actinobacillus pleuropneumoniae serovar 2 reference strain (S1536) and 5 serovar 2 field isolates with rabbit antiserum against S1536. Lanes: 1, atypical isolate OT761; 2, S1536; 3–6, field isolates A, B, C and D, respectively. Lane M=molecular mass marker in kilodaltons (kDa).). A similar pattern also occurred with the four isolates (A–D) but only two fast-migrating bands were seen in the LPS preparation of isolate OT761, a pattern typical of the R-LPS (Fig. 5).

Previous studies showed that the insertion of DNA sequences, such as ISApl1 or mini-Tn10, into one of the genes involved in biosynthesis of CPS and/or O-PS, as well as the deletion of DNA sequences in the cps and/or o-ps genes are responsible for the absence of CPS and/or O-PS, respectively, of A. pleuropneumoniae [10, 13, 27]. Our current study showed that a 93 bp deletion at the 3′ end of wcaJ gene in O-PS locus was correlated with the defect in the production of O-PS in isolate OT761. The sequence deletion in turn results in the absence of 31 amino acids at the C-terminus of WcaJ of isolate OT761 as compared to WcaJ of serovar 2 reference strain S1536 and the other isolates of serovar 2. WcaJ belongs to a prokaryotic family of membrane enzymes, termed polyisoprenyl-phosphate hexose-1-phosphate transferases (PHPTs), that catalyze the formation of a phosphoanhydride bond joining a hexose-1-phosphate with undecaprenylphosphate, which is the first step of O-PS biosynthesis [4, 20].

The mechanism by which the absence of 31 amino acids from the C-terminus of WcaJ results in the absence of O-PS was not defined in the present study. Nevertheless, some previous literature reported that the C-terminal domain of Salmonella Typhimurium WbaP, Escherichia coli WcaJ and Caulobacter crescentus HfsE, PssY and PssZ enzymes, members of the PHPT family, is sufficient for catalytic activity and specificity for initiating the biosynthesis of capsule [4, 20], and O antigen [18]. Data from previous reports about the structure-function of this enzyme family [4, 19] showed that the C-terminal domains and their secondary structures are conserved in distinct PHPT family members necessary to maintain the enzymatic activity of the C-terminal domains in the cytosolic space, where sugar nucleoside diphosphate precursors become available. It is expected that alterations of the secondary structure of truncated C-terminal domain of OT761 WcaJ might results in a loss of enzyme activity that impairs biosynthesis of O-PS. However, further studies would be needed to elucidate whether the C-terminus of A. pleuropneumponiae WcaJ play a key role in enzyme function involved in the biosynthesis of O-PS.

By SDS-PAGE and immunobloting, LPS of isolate OT761 lacked a ladder-like banding pattern typical of S-LPS in SDS-PAGE gels which is indicative of R-LPS. In immunoblotting, rabbit hyperimmune serum against strain S1536 reacted only with two fast-migrating bands corresponding to the core and lipid A structures indicating the isolate lost the O-PS. Our observation that higher temperature is liable to destroy the capsular antigen used for serotyping in A. pleuropneumoniae isolates is consistent with data from a recent report by Morioka et al. [17]. Those investigators found that some A. pleuropneumoniae isolates of serovars 1 and 15 were serotypable using the heat-extracted antigens (100°C, 1 hr) but UT using the autoclaved antigens (121°C, 1 hr) in AGD test. The very weak reactions between the whole-cell and heat-treated antigens and antiserum against serovar 2 strain S1536 were observed by the RSA test. The findings, together with data reported by other investigators [16, 25] support the idea that the agglutination tests are impossible to serotype the rough-type strain.

The loss of O-PS in vitro is considered to be due to mutations in culture, or by successive passages in different culture media. The 18-hr-old colonies of isolate OT761 on the chocolate agar plate at just after isolation (the first passage) were suspended in Brain Heart Infusion broth containing 15% glycerol, and stored at −80°C. Bacterial cells from the glycerol stock were used to streak onto chocolate agar plates to prepare the second passage (at 25th day after isolation) and then the third passage (at 26th day after isolation). The bacterial cells from the second and third passages were used to prepare DNA for further analysis, and antigens for the AGD test, respectively. The data provide very clear evidence that the loss of O-PS of isolate OT761 is not due to culture conditions and/or several culture passages.

The absence of serological cross-reactivity between the serovars 2 (strain S1536) and 7 (reference strain WF83) indicated that the AGD test detected antibodies directed against CPS and O-PS antigens, but not the core LPS epitopes. It has been well known that the core regions of A. pleuropneumoniae serovars 2–5, 7, 8, 10, and 12 were antigenically similar to each other as detected by immunoblot assays [11].

Our antigenic and genetic analyses of the atypical isolate revealed a 93 bp deletion at the 3′ end of wcaJ gene in the O-PS locus, resulting in the absence of O antigens. In practical application, the most commonly used test for detection of antibodies to A. pleuropneumoniae in the last 20 years has been enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and among serotype/serogroup-specific antigens, purified LC-LPS antigen (LC-LPS ELISA) has been the most successful [5]. Veterinary diagnostic laboratories should be vigilant, given that infections caused by such an isolate will not be detected by serologic tests based on LC-LPS.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest associated with this manuscript.

ack.p: The authors would like to thank Dr. C. Turni (University of Queensland, Australia) for critical review of the manuscript, Dr. T. Sato and Ms. H. Kojima (Nippon Institute for Biological Science) for their help during this work.
